quick_facts.label: Credential and typical format
quick_facts.value: M.Plan (Master of Planning) is a postgraduate planning degree. At the School of Planning and Architecture (SPA), New Delhi, its Urban Planning, Housing, and Transport Planning M.Plan programmes are listed as two-year programmes.

quick_facts.label: Common specialisations
quick_facts.value: Specialisations differ by institution. SPA New Delhi publishes M.Plan pathways in Environmental Planning, Housing, Regional Planning, Transport Planning, and Urban Planning.

quick_facts.label: Entry background
quick_facts.value: B.Plan and B.Arch are directly relevant feeder qualifications, but eligibility is institution- and specialisation-specific. SPA New Delhi’s published M.Plan criteria also accept selected engineering and social-science degrees for particular specialisations, subject to minimum marks.

quick_facts.label: Admission basis
quick_facts.value: At SPA New Delhi, the published postgraduate selection process considers qualifying-examination performance, a valid GATE/CEED/UGC-NET score where applicable, portfolio/thesis/project, interview, statement of purpose, and work experience. Applicants should treat this as an institution-specific example and check the current admission notice.

quick_facts.label: Illustrative public-institution cost
quick_facts.value: For SPA New Delhi’s 2024–25 fee schedule for Indian postgraduate students, general-category tuition was listed as ₹45,000 per semester and SC/ST tuition as ₹22,500 per semester, with additional institutional and optional hostel charges. This is dated 2024–25 information, not a national fee benchmark.

sections.heading: Eligibility: B.Plan and B.Arch are strong routes, but not the only ones
paragraphs: An M.Plan is commonly a next step after B.Plan or B.Arch, particularly for applicants seeking advanced work in urban, regional, housing, environmental, or transport planning. However, the exact qualifying degree, marks threshold, and accepted disciplines are set by each institution and can vary by specialisation.

SPA New Delhi’s published criteria require at least 55% aggregate or equivalent credits for general applicants and 50% for SC/ST/OBC applicants. For example, its Urban and Regional Planning routes accept planning, architecture, civil/architectural engineering and, for some routes, relevant master’s degrees in geography, economics, or sociology. Its Environmental Planning route additionally lists environmental engineering and selected environmental/social-science master’s qualifications. Applicants should verify the live notice for the target institution rather than assume that every M.Plan accepts the same background.
bullets: For an applicant with B.Plan or B.Arch: identify the intended planning specialisation, then compare its current eligibility rule and required documents.

For applicants from engineering or social-science backgrounds: check whether the specific specialisation explicitly lists the degree; eligibility can be narrower than the generic title “M.Plan” suggests.
Confirm the applicable marks rule, category provisions, score requirements, portfolio expectations, and whether final-year applicants may apply.

sections.heading: Duration
paragraphs: A full-time M.Plan is commonly structured as a two-year postgraduate programme. SPA New Delhi explicitly identifies its M.Plan programmes in Urban Planning, Housing, and Transport Planning as two-year programmes. The academic structure, internship requirements, and maximum time to complete the degree should nevertheless be checked in the rules of the admitting university.
bullets: Typical study pattern: two academic years.
Named specialisation and programme rules matter: M.Plan (Urban Planning), M.Plan (Housing), and M.Plan (Transport Planning) can have different course and studio emphases.

sections.heading: Curriculum: applied planning, analysis, studios, and thesis work
paragraphs: M.Plan curricula are specialisation-led and combine planning theory with applied analysis and studio-based work. SPA New Delhi’s Urban Planning syllabus, for example, includes city and metropolitan planning, infrastructure planning, sustainable planning and development, project planning and management, quantitative methods and GIS applications, land-use and transport planning, governance, development finance, planning legislation, studios, research seminar, and a final thesis.

The applied focus is visible across specialisations. SPA New Delhi describes its Urban Planning programme as using GIS, project planning, information technology, and studios based on real-world urban-development cases. Its Housing programme combines lectures, field assignments, and practical studios addressing housing policy, finance, infrastructure, informal settlements, GIS, real-estate project formulation, and governance. Its Transport Planning programme covers urban transport, public transport, traffic engineering, highway planning and design, transport economics, freight, road safety, intelligent transport systems, and policy.
bullets: Common capabilities developed: spatial and policy analysis, data and GIS use, plan preparation, infrastructure assessment, project formulation, research, professional communication, and implementation thinking.
Likely learning modes: taught courses, studios, fieldwork or case studies, electives, research seminar, and thesis.

Choose the specialisation based on the problem domain you want to work in—such as urban development, housing, regional development, environment, mobility, or transport systems—not only on the generic M.Plan title.

sections.heading: Admission: monitor institution-specific notices and prepare a planning portfolio
paragraphs: There is no single all-India M.Plan admission route. Institutions prescribe their own application calendars, eligibility conditions, entrance-score treatment, interviews, and documents. SPA New Delhi’s published process illustrates a multi-factor assessment: shortlisted candidates attend an in-person interview, and selection considers qualifying performance, valid GATE/CEED/UGC-NET score, portfolio/thesis/project, statement of purpose, and relevant work experience.

For the published SPA process, an applicant can apply to only one programme, and the page lists a non-refundable application fee of ₹2,500 for Indian nationals. These details are from SPA’s published postgraduate admissions information and may change in a later admission cycle; do not rely on historical dates, fees, or seat numbers without checking the current notification.
bullets: Build an application file early: marksheets, degree/provisional certificate, category documents if relevant, scorecard if required, CV, portfolio or thesis/project material, and statement of purpose.
Match the portfolio to the specialisation: show planning analysis, maps/GIS, studio outputs, research, policy work, fieldwork, design or infrastructure projects as relevant.
Use only the current notice of the chosen institution for deadlines, intake, score validity, interview format, and reservation rules.

sections.heading: Costs: use current institutional schedules, not a national average
paragraphs: M.Plan fees vary substantially by institution, applicant category, domicile or sponsorship status, hostel choice, and the academic session. As a dated public-institution example, SPA New Delhi’s fee notice dated June 21, 2024 for the 2024–25 session lists postgraduate tuition for Indian students at ₹45,000 per semester for the general category and ₹22,500 per semester for the SC/ST category. It separately lists enrolment, registration, academic-support, student-activity, refundable security-deposit, and hostel charges.

This example should not be projected to 2026–27 or treated as a typical private-college fee. Compare the latest fee notice and hostel/mess charges for every shortlisted institution, and separately budget for software, printing, fieldwork, travel, laptop/GIS needs, accommodation, and living expenses.
bullets: Dated SPA New Delhi 2024–25 illustration: general-category postgraduate tuition ₹45,000 per semester; SC/ST postgraduate tuition ₹22,500 per semester.

SPA’s same notice lists a refundable postgraduate school-and-library security deposit of ₹20,000 at admission and annual hostel room rent/electricity totalling ₹28,000 before the listed refundable hostel security; these figures are historical and may have changed.

SPA’s published postgraduate admissions page also states that limited merit scholarships may be available to non-sponsored postgraduate students with a valid GATE score, subject to government and school terms.

sections.heading: Next options after M.Plan
paragraphs: An M.Plan can lead to planning and related roles in public agencies, development authorities, urban local bodies, consulting, research, NGOs, real-estate and infrastructure organisations, and international-development settings. SPA New Delhi’s Urban Planning department reports alumni working with central and state town-and-country-planning organisations, development authorities, urban local bodies, international agencies, developers, consultants, research institutes, and NGOs; this is an institutional example rather than a placement guarantee.

For further study, relevant next steps can include a PhD in planning or an allied field, subject to each university’s doctoral eligibility and selection process. SPA New Delhi lists PhD programmes alongside its M.Plan offerings. Specialised professional development can also build depth in GIS and spatial analysis, urban policy, transport modelling, housing and real estate, climate resilience, infrastructure, or public finance.
bullets: Public-sector and statutory planning work: town and country planning organisations, development authorities, and urban local bodies.
Private and third-sector work: planning and urban-design consultancies, developers, infrastructure and mobility organisations, NGOs, and research bodies.
Academic/research route: pursue a PhD where eligible, or combine planning practice with research, teaching, policy, and technical specialisation.
